sentences.grammarNote: Allô is used exclusively when answering or opening a phone call — you would never greet someone in person with allô. In person, bonjour is always used.

sentences.base_language: Yes, it's me! How are you?
sentences.target_language: Oui, c'est moi ! Comment tu vas ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**C'est moi** = it's me — French uses the disjunctive/stressed pronoun **moi** after **c'est**, not *c'est je*. The same applies to other persons: c'est lui, c'est elle, c'est nous, c'est eux.
**Comment tu vas ?** is the informal version; the formal equivalent uses inversion: Comment allez-vous ?

sentences.base_language: I'm good, thanks. What are you doing this weekend?
sentences.target_language: Je vais bien, merci. Qu'est-ce que tu fais ce week-end ?
sentences.grammarNote: Qu'est-ce que tu fais is a common conversational question structure. The more formal inversion (Que fais-tu ?) is grammatically correct but sounds stiff in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: I don't have any plans yet.
sentences.target_language: Je n'ai pas encore de plans.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ne … pas encore** = not yet — **encore** is placed after **pas** to mean 'yet' in a negative sentence: je n'ai pas encore mangé (I haven't eaten yet).
Note: after a negative, **des** changes to **de**: des plans → **de** plans · des amis → **de** amis.

sentences.base_language: Do you want to go to a café on Saturday?
sentences.target_language: Tu veux aller dans un café samedi ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tu veux** + infinitive = do you want to …? — present tense of **vouloir** used as a question by intonation alone (no inversion needed in informal speech).
**dans un café** = to/in a café — **dans** is used for enclosed spaces; compare **au café** (at the café — using the contracted article).

sentences.base_language: Yes, I'd love to! Which one?
sentences.grammarNote: Lequel (which one) is an interrogative pronoun that agrees in gender and number with the noun it replaces: lequel (m. sg.), laquelle (f. sg.), lesquels (m. pl.), lesquelles (f. pl.).

sentences.base_language: The one near the Bastille. You know it?
sentences.target_language: Celui qui est près de la Bastille. Tu connais ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Celui qui** = the one that/which — **celui** is a demonstrative pronoun (masculine singular) replacing a masculine noun. Forms: celui (m. sg.), celle (f. sg.), ceux (m. pl.), celles (f. pl.).
**Tu connais ?** = Do you know it? — **connaître** (to know a place/person) vs **savoir** (to know a fact): je connais Paris · je sais qu'il pleut.

sentences.base_language: Yes, of course! At what time?
sentences.target_language: Oui, bien sûr ! À quelle heure ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**À quelle heure ?** = At what time? — **quelle** agrees with **heure** (feminine): quelle heure, quel jour, quels horaires.
**bien sûr** = of course — a very common filler/response. Alternatives: **évidemment**, **naturellement**, **absolument**.
Vers (around/about) is used for approximate times, just as in English: vers midi, vers dix-huit heures. For an exact time use à: à trois heures précises.

**À samedi !** = See you Saturday! — **à** + day/time is a common leave-taking formula: à demain (see you tomorrow), à tout à l'heure (see you soon/later), à lundi (see you Monday).
**Parfait** is an invariable response word meaning 'perfect' — does not agree with any noun here.

**À tout à l'heure** = see you in a little while / see you later (implies you will see the person later the same day).
Distinguish: **à tout à l'heure** (same day, soon) · **à plus tard** (later, vaguer) · **à demain** (tomorrow) · **à bientôt** (see you soon, timeframe unspecified).
**Salut** = hi or bye (informal, used both ways).
